h1Title: Occupational Asthma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Occupational asthma is adult-onset asthma caused or made worse by exposures at work, and it is the most common occupational lung disease in industrialized countries, accounting for an estimated 9-15% of all adult-onset asthma. Workers in healthcare, baking, woodworking, spray painting, cleaning, hairdressing, and agriculture are most affected; more than 400 high-molecular-weight (flour, animal proteins, latex) and low-molecular-weight (isocyanates, persulfates, glutaraldehyde, metal salts) sensitizing agents are documented. Patients present with cough, wheeze, chest tightness, and dyspnea that worsen during work shifts and improve on weekends or holidays. Early diagnosis and removal from exposure within the first 12 months produce remission in 30-50% of cases; continued exposure leads to persistent airway remodeling, severe asthma, and disability. Diagnosis combines serial peak flow records at and away from work with specific IgE, methacholine challenge, and where available, specific inhalation challenge.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 9-15% of all adult-onset asthma is occupational (ERS/ATS task force estimates); annual incidence 250-300 cases per million workers per year (UK SWORD data)
keyStats.demographics: Slightly higher in men (occupational exposure patterns); peak age 25-50; women predominate in cleaning and healthcare sub-types
keyStats.avgAge: Typical age of onset 25-50 years; latency from first exposure varies (months to years for sensitizer-induced, immediate for irritant-induced)
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1.4-2 million workers globally with current occupational asthma (Global Burden of Disease modeling)

definition: Occupational asthma (ICD-10: J45.0 in extrinsic asthma with sensitizing agent) is asthma caused or aggravated by exposure to agents encountered in the workplace. The American Thoracic Society and European Respiratory Society jointly classify it into two main forms: sensitizer-induced occupational asthma (allergic, immunologically mediated, latency period from months to years between first exposure and symptom onset) and irritant-induced occupational asthma (non-allergic, includes the classic reactive airways dysfunction syndrome (RADS) following a high-level acute exposure, and the related entity of low-dose irritant-induced asthma after repeated lower-level exposures). Sensitizer-induced disease accounts for roughly 90% of cases and is further divided by molecular size: high-molecular-weight agents (flour, animal dander, latex, enzymes) work through classical IgE-mediated mechanisms with positive skin-prick tests and specific IgE; low-molecular-weight agents (isocyanates, persulfates, anhydrides, metal salts, cleaning chemicals) often work through specific IgE plus T-cell-mediated mechanisms and may not be detectable on standard skin testing. A separate but related entity, work-exacerbated asthma, describes pre-existing asthma worsened by workplace irritants without development of new sensitization. Occupational asthma is managed by pulmonologists, occupational physicians, allergists, and primary care, often coordinated with workplace health and safety teams.

typesClassification.description: Latent immunologic sensitization to a specific workplace agent. High-molecular-weight agents (flour, animal proteins, latex) produce IgE-mediated reactions. Low-molecular-weight agents (isocyanates, glutaraldehyde, persulfates, anhydrides, plicatic acid in western red cedar) may act through IgE or non-IgE mechanisms. Latency 6 months to several years.

Irritant-induced asthma developing within 24 hours of a single, high-level inhalation of an irritant (chlorine spill, ammonia release, smoke inhalation). Asthma symptoms and bronchial hyperresponsiveness persist for at least 3 months.

Asthma developing after repeated lower-level exposure to airway irritants — cleaning workers, healthcare staff, and food-processing workers are typical. Mechanism less clearly defined than RADS or sensitization but increasingly recognized as a distinct entity.

Pre-existing asthma worsened by workplace exposure to non-specific irritants (cold air, dust, fumes, exertion) without new sensitization. Common but often confused with true occupational asthma; distinguishing the two affects compensation and removal decisions.

primarySymptoms: Cough, wheeze, chest tightness, and shortness of breath that begin or worsen during work shifts and improve on weekends or holidays — the most useful diagnostic pattern (sensitivity 60-80%).
Symptoms typically appearing several hours into a shift or in the evening after work; some patients have nocturnal symptoms peaking 8-12 hours after the workplace exposure (late asthmatic response).
Rhinoconjunctivitis (sneezing, nasal congestion, itchy red eyes) that precedes or accompanies asthma symptoms in 70-90% of patients with high-molecular-weight sensitizer-induced disease.
Urticaria, eczema flare, or angioedema on exposed skin (face, hands, neck) suggesting concurrent dermal sensitization.
Exercise tolerance reduced during work weeks and recovered during periods away from work.
Progressive worsening of symptoms over months despite symptomatic asthma treatment when exposure continues.
Acute severe bronchospasm shortly after high-level irritant exposure (chlorine spill, fume release, smoke inhalation) — RADS presentation.
earlyWarningSigns: New-onset cough or chest tightness in an adult worker, especially in high-risk industries (baking, cleaning, healthcare, woodworking, spray painting)
Symptoms that consistently improve during holidays or paid leave but return within days of resuming work
Rhinitis and conjunctivitis that flare at work — often precedes asthma symptoms by months
Reduced morning or post-shift peak expiratory flow on a personal peak-flow meter
Co-workers reporting similar symptoms — cluster of cases suggests a workplace exposure
emergencySigns: Severe acute breathlessness with audible wheeze unresponsive to inhaler — possible severe acute exacerbation; call emergency services
Cyanosis, inability to speak in full sentences, peak flow below 50% personal best, or pulse oximetry below 92% on room air — escalate to nebulized bronchodilator and oxygen
Massive irritant exposure (chlorine, ammonia, smoke) with rapid onset breathlessness — remove from area immediately, give oxygen, transfer to emergency department
Angioedema with facial or lip swelling, stridor, or hypotension — anaphylaxis with airway compromise, give intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg
Pneumothorax (sudden chest pain, breathlessness, unilateral chest signs) complicating a severe acute attack

causes.description: Flour (bakers, millers), animal proteins (laboratory workers, veterinarians, farmers), natural rubber latex (healthcare workers), enzymes (detergent industry), and seafood proteins (food processing) bind IgE and trigger classical allergic asthma. The latency from first exposure to symptoms is typically 6 months to 5 years.

Diisocyanates (TDI, MDI, HDI) in polyurethane manufacturing and spray painting are the single most reported cause globally. Other agents: persulfates in hairdressing, glutaraldehyde in healthcare, plicatic acid in western red cedar, acid anhydrides in epoxy resin, metal salts (chromium, nickel, cobalt), pharmaceutical dust, and reactive dyes.

Acute high-level inhalation of chlorine, ammonia, hydrochloric acid, sulfur dioxide, smoke, ozone, or combustion products can trigger reactive airways dysfunction syndrome — persistent airway hyperresponsiveness and asthma without prior sensitization. Onset is within 24 hours and symptoms persist for at least 3 months.

Cleaning workers exposed to mixed cleaning agents, healthcare workers using disinfectants (quaternary ammonium compounds, chlorhexidine), and food-processing workers show elevated asthma incidence without classical sensitization. Mechanism likely involves chronic epithelial injury and neurogenic inflammation.

Workers with pre-existing asthma may experience exacerbations from cold air, dust, fumes, smoke, allergens, or exertion in the workplace without acquiring new sensitization. Distinguished from true occupational asthma by absence of new sensitizer-specific responses but functionally important and compensable in many jurisdictions.

riskFactors.description: Healthcare, baking, cleaning, hairdressing, spray painting, woodworking, agriculture, laboratory animal handling, plastics manufacturing, and welding all carry elevated risk. UK SWORD data show bakers and healthcare workers among the highest-incidence groups.
Atopic individuals have 2-3 fold increased risk of high-molecular-weight sensitizer-induced asthma. Atopy is less predictive for isocyanate or other low-molecular-weight chemical-induced disease.

Smoking increases sensitization risk to several occupational allergens (flour, platinum salts, anhydrides) and worsens severity of disease once established. It is the single most important modifiable factor in many workplaces.
Higher cumulative dose and peak airborne concentrations increase incidence. Workplace engineering controls and personal protective equipment that reduce exposure substantially reduce risk.

Cleaning-related and healthcare-related asthma show higher rates in women, partly reflecting workforce sex distribution. Adjusted analyses still suggest a slightly higher hyperresponsiveness vulnerability in women.

Several HLA-DR alleles modify susceptibility to isocyanate-induced asthma, western red cedar asthma, and platinum-salt asthma. Glutathione S-transferase polymorphisms influence response to oxidative inhalation injury.
Occupational rhinitis precedes occupational asthma in 70-90% of high-molecular-weight cases and is itself a sentinel marker for high-risk workers.

affectedDemographics: Bakery workers: cumulative incidence of 5-15% over 5-10 years of exposure; flour and amylase are dominant sensitizers.
Healthcare workers: latex, glutaraldehyde, methacrylate cements, quaternary ammonium disinfectants — incidence 1-3 per 1,000 worker-years.
Spray painters and polyurethane manufacturing: isocyanate-induced asthma in 5-12% of exposed workers over a working lifetime.
Hairdressers: persulfate bleaches produce sensitization in 5-7% of workers within 5 years.
Cleaning workers: 25-50% increased asthma incidence versus general working population, with female cleaners particularly affected.
Agricultural workers: animal proteins, grain dust, mites, and pesticides; incidence varies by sub-sector.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis follows a structured stepwise approach codified in the ATS/ERS 2014 statement and similar national guidelines. Step 1 is to confirm asthma in the patient (variable airflow obstruction on spirometry with bronchodilator reversibility, or methacholine challenge with PC20 below 8 mg/mL when baseline FEV1 is normal). Step 2 is to establish a work-related pattern: a detailed occupational history capturing job tasks, exposures, latency, and a symptom pattern that worsens at work and improves away from work. Step 3 is to objectively document the work relationship: serial peak expiratory flow (PEF) measurements every 2 hours at and away from work for at least 4 weeks (2 at work and 2 away) showing greater diurnal variability or worse mean during work weeks. The Oasys software analyzes such PEF records and provides a validated work-effect score (specificity above 90% when the score is in the 'consistent' range). Step 4 identifies the specific sensitizer where possible: skin-prick tests and serum specific IgE for high-molecular-weight allergens (flour, animal dander, latex) and selected low-molecular-weight agents (isocyanates) are commercially available. Step 5, where feasible, is a specific inhalation challenge (SIC) in a specialized laboratory — the diagnostic gold standard. SIC exposes the patient to controlled doses of the suspected agent and measures FEV1 response; a fall of 20% or more confirms diagnosis. Fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) measurement, sputum eosinophil counts, and methacholine challenge before and after exposure provide additional support. Imaging is generally normal in occupational asthma; chest CT excludes alternative diagnoses such as hypersensitivity pneumonitis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms variable airflow obstruction — increase in FEV1 of 12% or more and 200 mL after a short-acting bronchodilator supports asthma
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient 30-minute test. You breathe forcefully into a mouthpiece before and 15 minutes after inhaling salbutamol. Results immediate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies airway hyperresponsiveness when baseline spirometry is normal; PC20 below 8 mg/mL supports asthma
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 60-90 minutes in a specialist lung-function lab. Stepwise inhalation of increasing methacholine concentrations with FEV1 measured after each dose. Mildly uncomfortable; reversed with salbutamol.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the work-relatedness of airflow variability; analyzed using Oasys software
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Use a personal peak-flow meter to record best of three blows every 2 hours during waking hours for at least 4 weeks, including 2 work and 2 away-from-work periods. Compliance must be careful for reliable interpretation.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin-prick tests and serum specific IgE
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies sensitization to specific high-molecular-weight allergens (flour, animal dander, latex) and selected low-molecular-weight agents
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Skin-prick: drops of allergen extracts placed on the forearm and pricked; results in 15-20 minutes. Specific IgE: blood draw, results within 5-7 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference standard. Controlled exposure to the suspected agent in a challenge chamber while monitoring FEV1; fall of 20% or more confirms diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 1-3 day admission at a specialized center. Exposure is gradually increased, with FEV1 measured before, during, and for 8 hours after. Available in tertiary occupational lung disease centers; not widely accessible.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Adjunctive marker of eosinophilic airway inflammation; can rise after work-related exposure in sensitizer-induced disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 1-minute exhalation into a handheld device; painless. Result immediate. Less specific than challenge testing but useful for monitoring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Eosinophilic phenotype supports allergic occupational asthma and predicts response to inhaled corticosteroid
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Induced sputum via inhaled hypertonic saline; collected and analyzed within hours. Available in specialist centers.

treatmentOverview: Treatment combines exposure control, pharmacotherapy, and management of co-morbidities. The cornerstone — and what distinguishes occupational asthma from other adult-onset asthma — is removal from the offending exposure. Workers diagnosed with sensitizer-induced asthma should be permanently removed from further exposure to the causal agent: continued exposure leads to persistent symptoms, deteriorating lung function, severe asthma, and disability. Removal achieves remission of asthma in 30-50% of cases, particularly when implemented within 12 months of symptom onset and when lung function is still well preserved. Where complete removal is impractical, substantial reduction with engineering controls, respirator use, and process modification can preserve some workers but is generally a less effective strategy and may delay rather than prevent disease progression. RADS and irritant-induced asthma require future avoidance of acute high-level exposure but do not necessarily require permanent removal from the workplace. Pharmacological treatment follows standard asthma stepwise approaches (GINA 2024): low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) alone or combined with formoterol as needed, escalating to ICS-LABA daily maintenance, then to medium- or high-dose ICS-LABA and add-on therapies (tiotropium, leukotriene receptor antagonist) for persistent symptoms. Severe eosinophilic occupational asthma benefits from biologic therapies — anti-IgE (omalizumab), anti-IL-5 (mepolizumab, reslizumab, benralizumab), or anti-IL-4/13 (dupilumab) — particularly in workers with persistent disease after removal. Coexisting rhinitis is treated with intranasal corticosteroid. Smoking cessation, treatment of obesity and GERD, and influenza and pneumococcal vaccination support overall control. Compensation and workplace surveillance are integral: most jurisdictions provide injury or occupational disease compensation, retraining, or relocation. Workplace medical surveillance programs — periodic spirometry and questionnaire screening of exposed workers — identify cases earlier and reduce long-term morbidity.

medicalTreatments.name: Removal from causative exposure
medicalTreatments.description: Permanent removal from the sensitizer is the single most effective intervention. Workers continuing exposure deteriorate progressively; those removed within 12 months of symptom onset have the best chance of remission. Substitution of safer agents (e.g. enzyme encapsulation, water-based paints) supports retained employment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Asthma remission in 30-50% of cases when complete removal occurs within 12 months of symptom onset; significantly higher persistence (60-80% chronic disease) if exposure continues

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) maintenance therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Low-to-medium dose ICS (budesonide 400-1,600 mcg/day or equivalent) reduces airway inflammation and symptoms. As-needed ICS-formoterol or daily ICS-LABA combinations are preferred over short-acting beta-agonist monotherapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces exacerbation rate by 50-60% and improves symptoms and quality of life in moderate persistent asthma (GINA stepwise data)

medicalTreatments.name: ICS-formoterol combination (budesonide-formoterol or beclomethasone-formoterol)
medicalTreatments.description: Single inhaler used both daily and as needed (SMART or MART regimen) is now preferred over salbutamol-only reliever therapy. Reduces severe exacerbation risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces severe exacerbation rate by 26-32% versus ICS plus separate short-acting beta-agonist (SYGMA trials)

medicalTreatments.name: Short courses of oral corticosteroid
medicalTreatments.description: Prednisolone 40-50 mg daily for 5-7 days used for acute exacerbations or severe persistent symptoms during diagnostic transition.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Substantial reduction in exacerbation duration and intensity; minimize cumulative use to limit systemic side-effects

medicalTreatments.name: Tiotropium add-on (long-acting muscarinic antagonist)
medicalTreatments.description: 5 mcg/day via soft-mist inhaler added in GINA step 4-5 for patients with persistent symptoms on ICS-LABA. Reduces exacerbation and improves FEV1.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces severe exacerbations by 20-30% and improves trough FEV1 in moderate-severe asthma

medicalTreatments.name: Biologic therapy (omalizumab, mepolizumab, benralizumab, dupilumab)
medicalTreatments.description: Targeted monoclonal antibodies for severe eosinophilic or allergic asthma, particularly in workers with persistent disease after removal. Selection based on biomarker phenotype (IgE, blood eosinophils, FeNO).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces severe exacerbations by 50-70% and oral steroid dependence by 50% in severe asthma populations

medicalTreatments.name: Allergen immunotherapy (selected cases)
medicalTreatments.description: Subcutaneous allergen immunotherapy has been used for hymenoptera-venom and animal-dander occupational asthma in selected workers when removal is impractical. Limited evidence; not first-line.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest improvement in some allergic phenotypes; not standard of care for most occupational asthma

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation programs
alternativeTreatments.description: Smoking cessation reduces asthma severity by 20-30% within 12 months and improves response to inhaled steroids. Combined behavioral support plus pharmacotherapy (nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion) is most effective.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured supervised exercise plus education improves exercise tolerance and quality of life in moderate-severe asthma. Reduces hospitalization rate by 20-30% in chronic patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Weight loss in obese workers
alternativeTreatments.description: 10-15% body weight reduction improves asthma control and reduces medication requirements in obese-asthma phenotype. Effect particularly pronounced in late-onset eosinophilic disease.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a pulmonologist or occupational physician at first suspicion of work-related asthma. Specialist input is essential for confirming the work association with serial PEF, specific IgE, and where available specific inhalation challenge, for advising on removal from exposure, and for coordinating workplace, compensation, and medical management. Severe or persistent disease warrants tertiary referral for biologic therapy consideration.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a pulmonologist with an occupational lung disease clinic, ideally one with access to specific inhalation challenge facilities and links to occupational medicine. Ask whether they perform serial PEF analysis, methacholine challenge, specific IgE, and biologic therapy selection. Occupational physicians are key partners and can advise on workplace controls, compensation, and return-to-work strategies.
hospitalCriteria: Pulmonary function laboratory with bronchial challenge testing capability
Access to specific IgE and skin-prick testing for occupational allergens
Multidisciplinary asthma clinic with pulmonology, allergy, occupational medicine, and respiratory physiology
keyFacilities: Tertiary occupational lung disease center with specific inhalation challenge capability
Severe asthma clinic offering biologic therapy
Occupational health and workplace surveillance program
preventionStrategies: Implement workplace engineering controls to minimize airborne exposure: local exhaust ventilation, process enclosure, substitution of safer agents.
Use appropriate respiratory protective equipment (powered air-purifying respirators or supplied-air systems) when engineering controls cannot adequately reduce exposure.
Participate in workplace medical surveillance: periodic spirometry, symptom questionnaires, and prompt assessment of any new respiratory symptoms.
Avoid smoking in workplaces handling sensitizing agents, as smoking compounds the risk.
Use enzyme encapsulation in detergent and food industries, water-based paints in spray painting, and low-allergen flour or amylase substitutes in baking where feasible.
Train workers on safe handling, decontamination, and emergency procedures for irritant releases.
Encourage early reporting of work-related respiratory symptoms — early diagnosis is the single best determinant of long-term outcome.
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking and avoid environmental tobacco smoke at work and home.
Use prescribed inhaled corticosteroid daily; correct technique with annual review.
Carry a quick-relief inhaler (ICS-formoterol or salbutamol) at all times during active disease.
Maintain an asthma action plan with personal best peak flow, color-coded zones, and step-up rules.
Limit exposure to general indoor air pollutants (dust mites, cockroach, pet dander, mold) that compound airway inflammation.
Get annual influenza vaccination and pneumococcal vaccination on schedule.
Manage co-morbidities: obesity, rhinitis, sleep apnea, GERD, and anxiety affect asthma control significantly.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet rich in fruits, vegetables, fish, olive oil, and nuts, linked to better asthma control in cohort studies
Omega-3 fatty acids from fatty fish or supplements (1-2 g/day)
Foods rich in vitamin D (fatty fish, fortified dairy) and supplementation to keep serum 25-OH-D above 30 ng/mL
Adequate intake of antioxidant vitamins (C and E) from whole foods rather than supplements
avoid: Sulfite-rich foods and drinks (wine, dried fruit, processed meats) in sulfite-sensitive patients
Specific food allergens documented to cause cross-reactivity (latex-fruit syndrome for healthcare workers)
Excessive alcohol, which worsens nocturnal symptoms and interacts with several asthma drugs
Highly processed foods low in micronutrients

exerciseGuidelines: Aerobic exercise — walking, swimming, cycling — for 30 minutes most days improves overall asthma control. Warm-up periods of 10-15 minutes reduce exercise-induced bronchoconstriction. Patients with exercise-induced symptoms should pre-treat with 2 puffs of ICS-formoterol or salbutamol 10-15 minutes before exercise. Avoid outdoor exercise during high pollen, high pollution, or extreme cold-air exposure. Supervised pulmonary rehabilitation is recommended for moderate-severe disease and after a major exacerbation.
dailyManagement: Take maintenance inhaler every day exactly as prescribed; check technique periodically with a respiratory nurse.
Monitor peak expiratory flow at home each morning (and at work during diagnosis or relapse) and act on the asthma action plan zones.
Avoid the offending sensitizer absolutely; report any inadvertent exposure to your occupational physician.
Wear prescribed respiratory protection in any environment where residual exposure may occur.
Maintain medication supply and have an emergency steroid course at home if recommended for severe disease.
Attend follow-up reviews every 3-6 months (or sooner if symptoms change) for spirometry, biomarker, and treatment adjustment.
prognosis: Outcome depends critically on time to diagnosis and exposure removal. Workers diagnosed and fully removed within 12 months of symptom onset achieve asthma remission in 30-50%; those removed within 24 months in 20-40%; beyond 24 months remission falls below 20%. Workers continuing exposure show progressive lung function decline (FEV1 fall of 50-100 mL per year) and develop severe persistent asthma in 60-80%. Quality of life and earning capacity are substantially affected — work loss, retraining, and disability claims are common. RADS often improves over 1-3 years but bronchial hyperresponsiveness can persist indefinitely. Severe occupational asthma with eosinophilic phenotype responds well to biologic therapy. Mortality is uncommon but possible during severe exacerbations; modern treatment has reduced asthma deaths overall. Workplace surveillance with periodic spirometry detects disease at earlier, more reversible stages.
recoveryTimeline: Symptom improvement after exposure removal begins within 4-12 weeks. Significant lung function recovery is usually apparent by 6-12 months. Airway hyperresponsiveness (methacholine PC20) improves over 12-24 months. Remission, when achieved, becomes evident over 1-3 years. RADS symptoms typically improve over 1-3 years but residual airway hyperresponsiveness can be permanent.
complications: Persistent severe asthma in 60-80% of workers who remain exposed beyond 24 months, with progressive FEV1 decline.
Severe acute exacerbations requiring hospitalization, intensive care, or rare fatal outcome.
Loss of employment, retraining requirements, and reduced earning capacity — major economic complication.
Anxiety, depression, and reduced quality of life associated with chronic respiratory symptoms and work disruption.
Bronchial fixed obstruction (irreversible airflow limitation) with chronic continued exposure, mimicking COPD physiology.
Co-existing allergic rhinitis, conjunctivitis, dermatitis, or anaphylaxis from the same sensitizer.

supportResources.name: British Occupational Health Research Foundation — Occupational Asthma Resources
supportResources.description: Evidence-based UK guidance on occupational asthma diagnosis and management.

supportResources.name: American Lung Association — Asthma
supportResources.description: US patient education, treatment guidance, and advocacy for asthma.

supportResources.name: Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) — Respiratory Hazards
supportResources.description: US workplace regulatory guidance on respiratory hazards and occupational asthma.

supportResources.name: Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA)
supportResources.description: International evidence-based asthma management guidelines updated annually.

confusedWithConditions.name: Non-occupational (Adult-Onset) Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both share symptoms of variable airflow obstruction. Occupational asthma is caused or aggravated by a workplace exposure with a clear work-related pattern (worse at work, better away). Distinguishing the two requires occupational history, serial peak-flow at and away from work, and specific IgE or inhalation challenge — and the distinction matters because removal from exposure is the cornerstone of occupational asthma treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: COPD is fixed airflow limitation usually from smoking or chronic dust/fume exposure, with progressive dyspnea and limited reversibility (under 12% FEV1 increase after bronchodilator). Occupational asthma shows variable airflow obstruction with significant reversibility and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. COPD progresses despite removal; occupational asthma improves.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (e.g. farmer's lung)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis produces fever, breathlessness, cough, and crackles after exposure to organic antigens (moldy hay, bird droppings), with restrictive rather than obstructive spirometry, ground-glass and centrilobular nodules on HRCT, and serum precipitins. Occupational asthma is obstructive, lacks fever, and shows airway hyperresponsiveness.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vocal Cord Dysfunction (paradoxical vocal fold movement)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vocal cord dysfunction causes acute breathlessness, throat tightness, and stridor that mimic asthma but result from inappropriate adduction of the vocal cords on inspiration. Spirometry shows inspiratory flow-volume flattening; bronchodilators do not help. Laryngoscopy during episodes is diagnostic. The two can coexist with asthma.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Occupational asthma is a subtype of asthma; pharmacological management overlaps but exposure control is unique.

relatedConditions.name: COPD
relatedConditions.relevance: Differential diagnosis in older smokers with workplace exposure; can coexist as asthma-COPD overlap.

relatedConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares atopic background and frequently coexists with occupational asthma in healthcare and food-processing workers.

relatedConditions.name: Mesothelioma
relatedConditions.relevance: Another occupational lung disease — relevant in workers with asbestos exposure who also have respiratory symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequent comorbidity that worsens overall asthma control if untreated.

faqs.question: What is occupational asthma?
faqs.answer: Occupational asthma is adult-onset asthma caused or aggravated by exposures at work. Symptoms — cough, wheeze, chest tightness, breathlessness — worsen during work shifts and improve on weekends or holidays. More than 400 workplace agents have been identified as causes.

faqs.question: What jobs cause occupational asthma?
faqs.answer: High-risk jobs include baking (flour, amylase), healthcare (latex, glutaraldehyde, methacrylates), spray painting (isocyanates), woodworking (western red cedar plicatic acid), hairdressing (persulfates), cleaning (mixed chemicals, quaternary ammonium), laboratory animal handling, welding, and food processing.

faqs.question: How is occupational asthma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines a detailed occupational history, spirometry with bronchodilator reversibility, serial peak expiratory flow records at and away from work for at least 4 weeks, methacholine challenge, and specific IgE or skin-prick testing. The reference standard is a specific inhalation challenge in a specialized laboratory.

faqs.question: Can occupational asthma be cured?
faqs.answer: Permanent removal from the causative exposure within 12 months of symptom onset achieves asthma remission in 30-50% of workers. Continued exposure leads to chronic severe asthma in most cases. Early diagnosis and rapid exposure removal are the single most important determinants of cure.

faqs.question: What is RADS (Reactive Airways Dysfunction Syndrome)?
faqs.answer: RADS is asthma that develops within 24 hours of a single high-level exposure to an irritant (chlorine, ammonia, smoke) and persists for at least 3 months. It is an irritant-induced form of occupational asthma without classical sensitization and is treated with standard inhaled corticosteroid therapy.

faqs.question: Can you keep working with occupational asthma?
faqs.answer: Continuing exposure leads to progressive lung function decline and severe asthma in 60-80% of cases. Workers should be permanently removed from the causative agent. Where complete removal is impossible, substitution of safer agents, engineering controls, and respiratory protection can sometimes allow retained employment but at higher risk.

faqs.question: What is the difference between work-exacerbated asthma and occupational asthma?
faqs.answer: True occupational asthma is new asthma caused by a workplace agent (sensitizer or irritant). Work-exacerbated asthma is pre-existing asthma made worse by non-specific workplace triggers (cold, dust, exertion) without new sensitization. Distinguishing the two affects diagnosis, compensation, and removal decisions.

faqs.question: Can occupational asthma cause permanent lung damage?
faqs.answer: Continued exposure beyond 12-24 months commonly leads to persistent severe asthma, airway remodeling, and irreversible airflow limitation. Lung function declines at 50-100 mL FEV1 per year while exposed. Early diagnosis and removal are the only proven means to preserve lung function.

faqs.question: Are there compensation rights for occupational asthma?
faqs.answer: Most jurisdictions recognize occupational asthma as a compensable occupational disease. Worker's compensation typically covers medical care, lost earnings, and retraining. Specific schemes vary by country and state. Early documentation by an occupational physician supports successful claims.

faqs.question: What treatments are used for occupational asthma?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines exposure removal with standard asthma pharmacotherapy: inhaled corticosteroid, ICS-LABA combinations, leukotriene receptor antagonists, tiotropium for severe disease, and biologic agents (omalizumab, mepolizumab, benralizumab, dupilumab) for severe eosinophilic phenotypes. Coexisting rhinitis is treated with intranasal corticosteroid.

faqs.question: What is isocyanate-induced asthma?
faqs.answer: Isocyanate-induced asthma is the single most common cause of occupational asthma in many industrialized countries. Diisocyanates (TDI, MDI, HDI) used in polyurethane foam, spray paint, and adhesives sensitize workers over months to years. Diagnosis typically requires specific inhalation challenge or serum specific IgE.

faqs.question: How long after exposure do symptoms appear?
faqs.answer: Sensitizer-induced asthma has a latency of 6 months to several years between first exposure and symptom onset. Symptoms then typically worsen with continued exposure. Irritant-induced asthma (RADS) appears within 24 hours of a single high-level exposure.

faqs.question: Do face masks protect against occupational asthma?
faqs.answer: Simple surgical masks do not protect against most occupational sensitizers. Properly fitted N95 or P2 respirators provide partial protection; powered air-purifying respirators or supplied-air systems offer the best individual protection. Engineering controls (ventilation, enclosure) and substitution of safer agents remain the priority.

faqs.question: Can occupational asthma come back after removal from exposure?
faqs.answer: Once sensitized, workers retain specific immune memory and re-exposure to the same agent can trigger severe asthma symptoms even after years of remission. Permanent avoidance is recommended. New sensitization to other agents can also develop if exposure continues to different sensitizers.

faqs.question: Are workers screened for occupational asthma?
faqs.answer: Workplaces handling known sensitizers should run medical surveillance programs: pre-employment assessment, periodic respiratory questionnaires, spirometry, and prompt evaluation of any new respiratory symptoms. Surveillance detects disease early and substantially reduces long-term morbidity in cohort studies.

faqs.question: Does smoking worsen occupational asthma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Smoking increases the risk of sensitization to several occupational agents (flour, platinum salts, anhydrides), increases asthma severity once established, and worsens response to inhaled corticosteroid. Smoking cessation typically improves control within 12 months and is the single most important modifiable factor.

faqs.question: Can children develop occupational asthma?
faqs.answer: Occupational asthma is by definition acquired through adult workplace exposure. However, adolescent and young adult workers in apprenticeships (bakery, hairdressing, construction) can develop it and should be monitored. Parental occupational dust may rarely cause childhood asthma via take-home contamination.

faqs.question: Are biologic therapies useful for occupational asthma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Patients with severe eosinophilic occupational asthma persistent after exposure removal often respond well to anti-IL-5 agents (mepolizumab, benralizumab) or dupilumab. Omalizumab is used for IgE-mediated allergic phenotypes. Biologics reduce exacerbations by 50-70% and steroid dependence by approximately 50%.

faqs.question: How is occupational asthma different from COPD?
faqs.answer: COPD shows fixed airflow obstruction with minimal reversibility, typically from smoking, and progresses despite removal of exposure. Occupational asthma shows variable airflow obstruction with significant bronchodilator reversibility and bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and improves with exposure removal. The two can coexist as asthma-COPD overlap.

faqs.question: What workplace controls reduce occupational asthma risk?
faqs.answer: Substitution of safer agents, enclosed processes, local exhaust ventilation, automated handling, regular cleaning, respiratory protection, and worker training reduce risk. Medical surveillance with periodic spirometry and prompt response to early symptoms further reduces disease incidence and severity.

faqs.question: What should I do if I think my asthma is caused by work?
faqs.answer: Tell your primary care doctor and your workplace occupational health team. Document symptoms in relation to work and time off. Request referral to a pulmonologist or occupational physician for serial peak-flow measurement, spirometry, and specific testing. Early diagnosis and exposure removal substantially improve long-term outcome.

metaTitle: Occupational Asthma: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
